name: Solare
classification_notes: Solare blends sans and serif characteristics in a fluid, expressive manner that leans toward humanist sans, with optical warmth and calligraphic influence evident across its weight range and italic styles.
summary: A variable humanist sans from Nikolas Wrobel with five weights, matching italics, an 'Intense' stylistic axis and upright Japanese support, released after five years of development.
description: Spanning sixteen static styles plus variable fonts, Solare offers Fine through Black in both upright and italic, with an additional Intense variant that adds contrast and visual weight across the range. The family is built for broad application across editorial, branding and digital contexts, with 748 glyphs covering 32 languages including Latin Extended and Japanese. A collection-exclusive display variant, Solare Sunset, is available to buyers of the full package.
foundry_blurb: Nikolas Type describe Solare as their grandest production yet, five years in the making. Designer Nikolas Wrobel sought to express the beauty of human experience through expressive letterforms, blending tradition with contemporary sensibility, sans with serif. The full collection includes variable fonts for fluid weight and style transitions, the glamorous Intense styles, entrancing alternates, Japanese support and the exclusive Solare Sunset display variant.

name: Grand Slang
classification_notes: Grand Slang merges grotesque and serif qualities drawn from mid-20th century American calligraphy traditions, placing it in a calligraphic serif subcategory with its non-modular, hand-influenced letterforms.
summary: A calligraphic serif by Nikolas Wrobel that translates mid-20th century American penmanship traditions into a sleek, reduced typeface merging grotesque and serif qualities.
description: Drawing on the calligraphic hands of Oscar Ogg and William A. Dwiggins, Grand Slang retranslates rather than recreates its historical sources, arriving at over 310 non-modular characters that blend the warmth of calligraphy with the economy of a grotesque. Three styles — Roman, Italic and the alternate B-Side — give designers flexibility across editorial, branding and packaging contexts. Its uptake by Nike, Mandarin Oriental and Gestalten confirms its standing as a versatile yet distinctly voiced display face.
foundry_blurb: Nikolas Type describe Grand Slang as boldly discovering and reshaping the essence of beautiful mid-20th century American calligraphy, passing on the warmth of former hands like Oscar Ogg and William A. Dwiggins. The font takes an idealistic approach to merging grotesques and serifs across more than 310 individual, non-modular upper and lowercase characters, ligatures, figures and signs, covering 32 languages.

name: Cosi Azure
classification_notes: Cosi Azure is a radically contrasted display typeface with delicate pointed forms set against bold irregular chunks, placing it firmly in the high-contrast display category rather than any conventional serif or sans tradition.
summary: A radically contrasted display typeface by Nikolas Wrobel, spinning off Cosi Times with jagged pointed forms and heavy irregular masses drawn from Mediterranean landscapes.
description: Released in 2019, Cosi Azure pairs delicate pointed letterforms against bold, uneven strokes in a deliberately anti-modular approach to type design. The four styles range from Bold through to a Black Stencil variant, giving the family an expressive range suited to editorial headlines and cultural branding. Its character comes from the tension between refined and rough, reportedly drawn from personal experience of Sardinia and Corsica.
foundry_blurb: Nikolas Type describe Cosi Azure as a radically contrasted offshoot of Cosi Times, inspired by the natural landscapes and spirit of Mediterranean islands Sardinia and Corsica. They say its delicate and pointed forms are counterpoised with bolder and wilder chunks, evoking the sweet air, pointed peaks and the soothing sound of the sea, with an authentic appearance born out of personal experience and offered as an antithesis to generic modular type design.

name: Cosi Times
classification_notes: An uppercase-only display serif with warm, humanist qualities, deliberately crafted without a rigid system to produce an intentionally imperfect, expressive character suited to headline use.
summary: An uppercase-only display serif by Nikolas Wrobel, kerned by hand to keep an intentionally imperfect, human quality.
description: Three weights plus a condensed cut make up this display-only family, restricted to uppercase and figures. Wrobel deliberately avoided a systematic approach to spacing and kerning, giving the typeface a warmth and irregularity that sets it apart from more mechanical display serifs. At 163 glyphs across 32 languages, it covers extended Latin for international headline work.
foundry_blurb: Nikolas Type describe Cosi Times as a celebration of typography, with Nikolas Wrobel leading a development process that reimagined warm, gentle memories and brought them into a modern, sleek and distinctive typeface. The result is a workable, uppercase display font crafted and kerned without a precise system, making it human, genuine and imperfect.
